localBiteReport: The most recent reports from Choctawhatchee Bay, dated within the past week, show speckled trout as the standout inshore species right now — charter reports from the Santa Rosa Beach area describe a strong and consistent trout bite, with scattered fish found throughout the bay and anglers landing solid specimens. Bull redfish are also active, with at least one notable catch logged near the Santa Rosa Beach shoreline. Flounder have appeared in the mix on recent charter trips out of Destin Bay. Wind has been a recurring factor over the past couple of weeks, pushing anglers to be selective about where they set up, but those who found protected water reported good action. Water temperature is 85°F. The Half Hitch Destin report blog posts through July 13, 2026 are visible on their site but the full text of those most recent reports was not accessible in search results.
localBiteSource: captainexperiences.com (Choctawhatchee Bay reports, 6 days ago and ~1 week ago); fishingbooker.com (Choctawhatchee Bay charter catch reports, July 2026) · Auto-refreshed via Claude API

aiSummary: Choctawhatchee Bay is fishing well this week, with speckled trout leading the charge across the bay and bull redfish showing up along the Santa Rosa Beach shoreline — but today's 71% storm chance with thunderstorms most likely before 1 PM demands an early start and a plan to be off the water well before midday. SSE winds at 15–20 mph will push chop across open water and favor anglers who target protected bayous and creek systems. Get lines in at first light, work fast, and have a bailout plan ready before that afternoon convective window closes in.

bridge.aiNote: The 331 Bridge spans a narrow neck of Choctawhatchee Bay, creating a natural current funnel that pushes bait through on both tides. Pilings hold speckled trout, redfish, sheepshead, and black drum year-round, with lee-side pilings serving as the prime ambush zone on incoming tides. This is exposed, open water — it's typically the first spot on the bay to become unfishable when wind builds.
bridge.todaysCall: With SSE winds running 15–20 mph, expect significant chop at the bridge and a rough ride to and from the structure — this spot may not be worth the exposure today. If you commit, target the lee-side pilings in the first hour of light before conditions deteriorate; the falling tide toward the 12:02 PM low will push bait through the pilings, but the incoming tide in the overnight hours already set up fish. Given the 71% storm chance with thunderstorms likely before 1 PM, plan to clear this exposed location no later than 10–11 AM. Slightly stained water and a cloudy sky actually favor trout here, so if wind is your only concern and it's manageable at launch, a short early window is worth targeting.

alaqua.aiNote: Alaqua Bayou offers protected water with a strong year-round redfish population, along with trout and flounder near the mouth. Oyster bars at the creek mouth are the prime target zone, and tree cover keeps water temperatures a few degrees cooler than the open bay while providing meaningful shelter from wind and weather. The upper creek transitions toward brackish, where bass mix in alongside smaller reds.
alaqua.todaysCall: Alaqua is a smart pick today — the tree-lined banks and protected geometry will knock down the SSE 15–20 mph wind significantly, and the oyster bars at the creek mouth should be productive as the outgoing tide builds toward the 12:02 PM low. Start at the mouth on the falling tide to work bait-holding current over those bars, then push into the upper creek as conditions and weather allow. Bay-wide reports are showing a strong trout bite and active redfish, both of which are core Alaqua species — confidence is high. With storms likely before 1 PM, you have a solid morning window; plan your exit by 11 AM to stay ahead of lightning risk on the run back.

basin.aiNote: Basin Bayou sees less fishing pressure than nearby Alaqua and LaGrange, which tends to translate to less-educated, more willing fish. The interior grass flats are classic sight-fishing territory for redfish in water under two feet, and pompano occasionally appear at the mouth on moving tides in late spring and early summer. It offers some shelter but is generally less protected than LaGrange when weather builds.
basin.todaysCall: Basin Bayou is a reasonable choice today for anglers willing to get there early, but keep in mind it's less protected than LaGrange if wind really stacks up from the SSE. The falling tide toward the 12:02 PM low is your primary window — work the mouth and any current-adjacent structure where bait concentrates, then push the interior flats for reds if clarity allows. At 85°F water temp, reds on the shallow flats will be most active in the first two hours after sunrise; the cloudy sky and slightly stained water are actually helpful for low-profile approaches. Commit to a hard exit by 10:30–11 AM given the 71% storm probability before 1 PM, and have a weather eye on the southwest sky throughout.

lagrange.aiNote: LaGrange Bayou is widely considered the top trout producer among the bayous on this side of the bay, with legendary oyster bars at the mouth and interior grass flats that hold redfish year-round. Deep holes inside hold black drum and sheepshead, and the bayou's geography makes it the most protected water in this part of the bay when weather builds. It's the default go-to when conditions deteriorate elsewhere.
lagrange.todaysCall: LaGrange is the call today — it's the most weather-protected spot on the dashboard and exactly what a day with SSE 15–20 mph wind and a 71% storm chance calls for. The outgoing tide toward the 12:02 PM low will create prime current flow over the oyster bars at the mouth; start there at first light targeting trout, which are the confirmed standout species bay-wide right now. As the bite shifts, move inside to the grass flats for reds or probe the deep holes for black drum and sheepshead. The slightly stained water and overcast sky are favorable for trout on topwater or shallow suspending baits early. Plan to be rigged and fishing by 6 AM, and off the water by 11 AM — the storm window opens before 1 PM and LaGrange gives you the shortest exposure getting in and out.

fourmile.aiNote: Four Mile Creek is a short tidal creek running between Shipyard Marina and the bay, making it both convenient and quick to fish. Incoming tides push baitfish up the creek, concentrating redfish and trout in the bends and along grass edges, while the creek mouth is a reliable flounder zone. The upper section transitions toward brackish water and holds bass alongside smaller reds. It's one of the safest fisheries on the bay when weather threatens.
fourmile.todaysCall: Four Mile Creek might be the most practical spot on today's dashboard — it's right off Shipyard Marina, and its short, sheltered layout means you can fish effectively and get off the water fast if storms develop early. The overnight high tide set bait up into the creek, and the falling tide toward the 12:02 PM low will push that bait back out through the bends and grass edges — prime time for trout and reds ambushing in current. Work topwater or shallow-running paddle tails through shaded bends early, then target the creek mouth for flounder as tide pulls stronger. Water is 85°F and slightly stained, which keeps fish tucked tighter to structure and edges. This is a great option for anglers who want to maximize time fishing while minimizing storm exposure risk.

hogtown.aiNote: Hogtown Bayou is a signature Santa Rosa Beach guide spot and one of the most consistently productive trout flats on Choctawhatchee Bay. Summer mornings are peak season, with trout stacking on grass in two to four feet of water, and Spanish mackerel appear at the mouth on moving tides. It sees regular pressure from guides and locals but produces reliably, and it's worth noting the longer run time from the 331 bridge area compared to other bayous.
hogtown.todaysCall: Hogtown is highly relevant today — bay-wide reports from the Santa Rosa Beach area specifically call out a strong, consistent trout bite, and this is that exact neighborhood. Target the grass flats in 2–4 feet right at first light, before boat pressure builds and before the SSE wind starts stacking up a chop on the run back. The falling tide toward noon will set up nice current at the mouth, and that's your window to check for Spanish mackerel with a fast-moving spoon or jig. Factor in the longer run from Shipyard Marina and plan accordingly — leave the ramp no later than 5:45–6:00 AM to maximize fishing time before the pre-1 PM storm window forces you off. Slightly stained water and cloud cover are working in your favor for the trout bite; don't overspin the presentation.

rocky.aiNote: Rocky Bayou sits inside Fred Gannon Rocky Bayou State Park near Niceville, offering a protected, scenic, and significantly less-pressured fishery than bayous closer to the 331 bridge. Freshwater influence from Rocky Creek makes flounder fishing excellent, and the glass-calm state park coves are ideal for sight-fishing redfish. Mangrove snapper hold near dock pilings, rounding out a diverse species mix. It is the furthest location from Shipyard Marina, so run time must be factored into the plan.
rocky.todaysCall: Rocky Bayou is a legitimate storm-day sanctuary — the state park setting is well-protected from SSE wind, and the glass-calm coves will be fishable when other spots are getting hammered by chop. The flounder bite here is tied to freshwater influence from Rocky Creek, and that dynamic doesn't change with today's conditions; work the creek mouth and transition zones on the falling tide toward the 12:02 PM low. Sight-fish the calm coves for reds early while light is low and the overcast sky reduces glare, and check dock pilings for mangrove snapper later in the morning. The big caveat today is the run — Rocky Bayou is the furthest spot on the dashboard, and with storms likely before 1 PM carrying a 71% probability, you need to be especially disciplined about departure time. Leave early, fish smart, and don't let the protected water lull you into staying past 10:30 AM.
